metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Biology 12 provides an in-depth study of the molecular and cellular foundations of life, building on Life Sciences 11. Students explore biochemistry, cell biology, genetics and molecular biology, and the physiology of human organ systems. The course develops scientific reasoning, experimental design, and data analysis skills at a senior level. First Peoples perspectives on living systems, ecological relationships, and the interconnectedness of life are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A senior biology course exploring molecular biology, cell function, genetics, and human physiology — developing scientific reasoning and preparing students for post-secondary life and health sciences.

big_ideas.statement: The cell is the basic unit of life and molecular processes within cells determine structure and function.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine how the biochemical organization of cells — from membranes to organelles to enzymes — underlies all biological processes, connecting molecular structure to cellular and organismal function.

big_ideas.statement: DNA and the mechanisms of heredity explain how traits are passed from one generation to the next.
big_ideas.elaboration: Grade 12 students explore the molecular basis of inheritance — DNA replication, transcription, translation, and mutation — and connect these mechanisms to patterns of inheritance and biotechnology applications.

big_ideas.statement: Organ systems are interdependent and work together to maintain homeostasis.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students analyze how the major organ systems of the human body — digestive, circulatory, respiratory, nervous, and endocrine — interact through feedback mechanisms to maintain a stable internal environment.

big_ideas.statement: Evolution by natural selection and other mechanisms explains the diversity and unity of life.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students connect population genetics, mechanisms of evolution, and evidence for evolution to explain both the diversity of life and the shared molecular and structural features across organisms.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate a sustained intellectual curiosity about a scientific topic or problem of personal interest; make observations aimed at identifying their own questions, including increasingly complex ones, about the natural world; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict multiple outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify and develop complex questions about biological systems and phenomena.
I can formulate testable hypotheses with multiple predicted outcomes based on biological knowledge.
I can connect my scientific curiosity to real-world biological problems in medicine, genetics, and ecology.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively and individually plan, select, and use appropriate investigation methods, including field work and lab experiments, to collect reliable data; assess risks and address ethical, cultural, and environmental issues associated with their proposed methods; use appropriate SI units and apply significant figures conventions; and record and organize data using a range of tools.
i_can_statements: I can independently design controlled experiments in biology, identifying variables and selecting appropriate methods.
I can apply ethical frameworks to biological research — particularly in genetics, human subjects, and environmental work.
I can collect and record precise biological data using laboratory instruments and digital tools.
I can apply significant figures, SI units, and proper lab safety protocols consistently.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Experience and interpret the local environment; apply First Peoples perspectives and knowledge, other ways of knowing, and local knowledge as sources of information; seek and analyze patterns, trends, and connections in data, including describing relationships between variables, and identifying inconsistencies; construct, analyze, and interpret graphs, models, and diagrams; use knowledge of scientific concepts to draw conclusions consistent with evidence.
i_can_statements: I can analyze biological data sets to identify patterns, trends, and anomalies.
I can construct and interpret graphs, molecular diagrams, and biological models accurately.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions from experimental results and connect them to biological theory.
I can incorporate First Peoples ecological knowledge as a valid lens for understanding living systems.

competencies.raw: Evaluate their methods and experimental conditions, including identifying sources of error or uncertainty, confounding variables, and limitations; describe specific ways to improve their investigation methods; evaluate the validity and limitations of a model; and demonstrate an understanding of the importance of peer review.
i_can_statements: I can critically evaluate my experimental methods, identify sources of error, and propose specific improvements.
I can assess the validity and limitations of biological models and simulations.
I can understand and apply the principles of peer review and scientific reproducibility.

competencies.raw: Contribute to care for self, others, and community through personal or collaborative approaches; transfer and apply learning to new situations; generate and introduce new or refined ideas when problem solving; and contribute to finding solutions to problems at a local and/or global level.
i_can_statements: I can apply biological knowledge to real-world problems in health, medicine, and environmental stewardship.
I can propose innovative, evidence-based solutions to biological and medical challenges.
I can transfer biological concepts to new contexts including biotechnology, public health, and conservation.

competencies.raw: Communicate ideas, findings, and solutions to problems using scientific language, representations, and digital technologies; express and reflect on a variety of experiences, perspectives, and worldviews through place.
i_can_statements: I can communicate biological ideas with precision using accurate scientific terminology and representations.
I can produce well-structured lab reports, research summaries, and scientific arguments.
I can use digital tools to model, visualize, and present biological data and concepts.

areas.name: Biochemistry
areas.raw: Biochemistry: organic molecules, enzymes, and cellular energy.
topics: Organic molecules: structure and function of carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids
Enzyme structure and function: active site, substrate specificity, and reaction rates
Enzyme regulation: inhibition (competitive and non-competitive), cofactors, coenzymes, and allosteric regulation
Cellular respiration: glycolysis, Krebs cycle, electron transport chain, and ATP production
Photosynthesis: light reactions, Calvin cycle, and the relationship between photosynthesis and respiration
Bioenergetics: ATP as energy currency and the coupling of exergonic and endergonic reactions

areas.name: Cell Biology
areas.raw: Cell biology: membrane structure, transport, and cell cycle.
topics: Cell membrane: fluid mosaic model, phospholipid bilayer, membrane proteins, and cholesterol
Membrane transport: diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion, active transport, endocytosis, and exocytosis
Cell communication: signal transduction pathways and receptor-ligand interactions
Cell cycle: interphase, mitosis (PMAT), cytokinesis, and cell cycle regulation
Cancer biology: mutations in cell cycle regulation, tumour suppressor genes, and oncogenes
Stem cells: types, properties, and ethical considerations in medical applications

areas.name: Molecular Genetics
areas.raw: Molecular genetics: DNA structure, replication, gene expression, and biotechnology.
topics: DNA structure: double helix, nucleotide structure, base pairing, and antiparallel strands
DNA replication: semi-conservative model, helicase, DNA polymerase, and Okazaki fragments
Transcription: RNA polymerase, promoters, mRNA synthesis, and post-transcriptional processing
Translation: ribosomes, codons, tRNA, and the genetic code
Gene regulation: operons (prokaryotes), transcription factors, and epigenetics (eukaryotes)
Mutations: types (substitution, insertion, deletion), causes, consequences, and repair
Biotechnology: PCR, gel electrophoresis, CRISPR-Cas9, cloning, and DNA sequencing
Genetic testing: applications, ethics, privacy, and social implications

areas.name: Human Physiology
areas.raw: Human physiology: organ systems, homeostasis, and health.
topics: Digestive system: organs, mechanical and chemical digestion, absorption, and nutrient metabolism
Circulatory system: heart structure and function, blood vessels, blood composition, and blood pressure regulation
Respiratory system: gas exchange, lung mechanics, hemoglobin, and regulation of breathing
Nervous system: neuron structure, action potentials, synaptic transmission, and CNS/PNS organization
Endocrine system: hormones, target cells, feedback regulation, and major glands
Immune system: innate and adaptive immunity, antibodies, vaccines, and autoimmune conditions
Homeostasis: feedback loops, thermoregulation, blood glucose regulation, and osmoregulation
Reproductive system: gametogenesis, fertilization, pregnancy, and hormonal regulation

areas.name: Evolution and Biodiversity
topics: Mechanisms of evolution: natural selection, genetic drift, gene flow, and mutation
Population genetics: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium and factors that disrupt it
Speciation: allopatric and sympatric speciation, reproductive isolation mechanisms
Evidence for evolution: fossil record, comparative anatomy, molecular biology, and biogeography
Phylogenetics: cladograms, evolutionary trees, and classification systems
Biodiversity and conservation: species diversity, ecosystem services, and extinction threats
First Peoples knowledge of biodiversity: traditional ecological knowledge and conservation stewardship
